terms.definition: An open-source, self-hosted framework for building autonomous AI agents that connect to messaging channels and can take real actions, not just answer questions.
terms.example: e.g. Running OpenClaw with a WhatsApp channel lets you message an agent that can read files, run commands, and search the web on your behalf.

terms.definition: An AI system that is given a goal and a set of tools, then independently decides which actions to take and in what order to reach that goal.
terms.example: e.g. An agent told to "monitor this inbox" keeps checking and drafting replies without being re-prompted for each new email.

terms.definition: A tool that wraps a foundation model with built-in functions but is invoked deliberately by a human for one task at a time.
terms.example: e.g. Claude Code running in a terminal is a harness — a developer triggers each action rather than the model acting continuously on its own.

terms.definition: OpenClaw's control plane — the single point through which agents, skills, channels, and settings are configured.
terms.example: e.g. All configuration for a new WhatsApp-connected agent happens by logging into the Gateway console.

terms.definition: A definition of how and when an agent should use a capability, stored as a `SKILL.md` file, without the underlying implementation code.
terms.example: e.g. A "web search" skill tells the agent to search online whenever it doesn't already know an answer.

terms.definition: The actual executable capability behind a skill — the code that performs an action such as reading a file or making an HTTP request.
terms.example: e.g. The tool behind a "web search" skill is the code that actually queries a search API.

terms.definition: The markdown file in OpenClaw that defines an agent's persona, tone, and behavioral boundaries.
terms.example: e.g. A SOUL.md file might instruct an agent to always reply in under 500 characters and never discuss unrelated topics.

terms.definition: A standardized protocol that lets an AI agent connect to external tools, data sources, and systems.
terms.example: e.g. An agent uses an MCP server to query a company's internal ticketing system without a custom integration being written for it.

terms.definition: The underlying large language model that provides an agent's reasoning and language capabilities.
terms.example: e.g. OpenClaw is model-agnostic, so the same agent configuration can run on Claude, GPT, or another model.

terms.definition: A fully managed, serverless AWS platform for building, deploying, and securing production AI agents at scale, with any framework or foundation model.
terms.example: e.g. A team migrates a self-hosted agent to AgentCore to get per-session isolation without managing EC2 instances.

terms.definition: The AgentCore component that provides a secure, serverless execution environment for an agent, billed for active CPU and memory usage only.
terms.example: e.g. A support agent's Runtime session spins up when a customer messages it and shuts down after the conversation ends.

terms.definition: The AgentCore component that turns APIs and Lambda functions into tools an agent can call, and connects to existing MCP servers.
terms.example: e.g. An internal inventory API is exposed through AgentCore Gateway so an agent can check stock levels.

terms.definition: The AgentCore component that manages an agent's short-term (in-session) and long-term (cross-session) memory without custom infrastructure.
terms.example: e.g. An agent remembers a returning customer's timezone and preferences across separate conversations using AgentCore Memory.

terms.definition: A managed, cloud-based Chromium browser runtime that lets an agent navigate and interact with live websites.
terms.example: e.g. A research agent uses AgentCore Browser to pull the latest market data from a live webpage.

terms.definition: The delay experienced the first time a serverless environment (such as an AgentCore Runtime session) has to initialize before it can respond.
terms.example: e.g. The first message to an idle agent takes a few extra seconds while its session environment spins up.

terms.definition: The architectural challenge of serving multiple customers or users from shared infrastructure while keeping their data and sessions isolated from one another.
terms.example: e.g. A SaaS company must ensure Customer A's agent conversations never appear in Customer B's session.

terms.definition: A multi-tenancy pattern where every user shares the same underlying resources, minimizing cost but weakening isolation.
terms.example: e.g. Ten customers all use the same EC2 instance and database in a pool deployment.

terms.definition: A multi-tenancy pattern where every user gets fully dedicated resources, maximizing isolation at higher cost.
terms.example: e.g. Each enterprise customer gets their own EC2 instance and database in a silo deployment.

terms.definition: A multi-tenancy pattern that shares some resources across users while isolating sensitive resources per user.
terms.example: e.g. All customers share a common Gateway front-end, but each has a dedicated agent session and data store.

terms.definition: An AWS Systems Manager capability that lets you connect to an EC2 instance through the AWS control plane using IAM permissions, without opening inbound port 22 or using SSH keys.
terms.example: e.g. An instance in a fully private subnet with no internet gateway can still be reached for troubleshooting via Session Manager.

terms.definition: AWS's resizable virtual server service, offering full control over the operating system and networking configuration.
terms.example: e.g. A team runs OpenClaw on an EC2 instance bootstrapped via CloudFormation user data.

terms.definition: A simplified AWS compute service with pre-built blueprints, designed for fast setup with less configuration than raw EC2.
terms.example: e.g. Launching OpenClaw from a Lightsail blueprint gets an instance running within minutes.

terms.definition: A configurable safety layer that can filter sensitive content and enforce topic restrictions on traffic flowing to and from a foundation model.
terms.example: e.g. Guardrails block a customer's financial account number from being sent to the LLM in a support conversation.

terms.definition: AWS's family of ARM-based processors, generally offering a lower cost-per-vCPU than comparable x86 instances for general-purpose workloads.
terms.example: e.g. Running OpenClaw on a Graviton-based EC2 instance can reduce compute cost versus an equivalent x86 instance.

terms.definition: An agent responsible for receiving a request, routing sub-tasks to specialized agents, and combining their responses into a single answer.
terms.example: e.g. An orchestrator agent routes a billing question to a billing-specialist agent and a technical question to a support-specialist agent.
